major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: The Bailong River basin is situated between the two major left-lateral strike-slip faults of the East Kunlun Fault and the West Qinling Fault. The unique geological conditions of this area have made it one of the most developed and severely affected regions by geological disaster chains in China, resulting in a highly severe disaster prevention and mitigation situation. This paper builds a database of 132 significant landslide disaster chains through data collection, remote sensing interpretation, and field surveys, systematically exploring their developmental distribution characteristics and formation mechanisms. The study indicates: (1) The landslide bodies are all of large scale or larger, showing significant scale effects, with landslide quantities in the range of (1000–5000) × 10³ m³ accounting for 40.2% and total volume accounting for 44.8%; predominantly characterized by high-position accumulation layer landslides, 24.2% exhibit a "three-stage" anti-slide topography feature, with landslides developed in the Devonian and Silurian soft-hard combinative easily sliding structural strata covering 65% of total landslide area. (2) Deformation mainly involves multi-stage retreating traction, with some locally accompanied by tensile-shear push, gradual shift-type fault zone landslides as well as composite large accumulation body landslides; over 60% demonstrate long-distance movement mechanisms, particularly characterized by long-lasting creep-dominated strong debris flow. (3) Spatially, these landslides show a banded concentration along major active fault zones and a linear distribution along river systems, with 54.6% occurring within 2 km of fault zones, predominantly concentrated at fault disjunctions, inflections, termini, and convergence areas; sliding directions are often parallel to fault orientations, with NWW-NW and NE faults jointly controlling area distribution with water systems. (4) Their formation and development are mainly controlled by active faults, easily sliding rock layers such as slate, and varying mid-high mountain canyon topography; regional 5-15 days of long-duration heavy rainfall are the primary triggering factors with a lag effect, and the frequency and scale of disasters significantly increase 1.5-5 years post-earthquake. The research findings play an important guiding role in enriching the understanding of the formation mechanisms of large and extraordinarily large landslide disaster chains in the northeastern edge of the Tibetan Plateau and enhancing disaster prevention and mitigation capabilities.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: Taking the landslide event in Liangshui Village, Zhenxiong County, Yunnan Province on January 22, 2024, as an example, a detailed analysis of the causes of the landslide was conducted using media information review, geographical environmental factor analysis, and comparative analysis methods. The results indicate: (1) The landslide shows a weak dependence on external triggering conditions, being significantly influenced by natural factors, categorizing it as a weak-trigger landslide. (2) The steep slopes and unstable gentle slope platforms near the landslide site constitute the fundamental conditions for the landslide occurrence, making it susceptible to collapse and sliding under gravitational influence. (3) The Triassic sandstone and mudstone interbedded with thin layers of mud shale are prone to softening, mudification, and seepage erosion along bedding planes, structural joints, or surface weathering fractures. Structural joints that vertically cut through the rock layers create an unstable interleaved framework; once displacement occurs, this layered jointed rock mass can rapidly develop into toppling deformation or even structural collapse. (4) Rainfall infiltration can erode solution cavities in the rock mass, soften the rock layers, and reduce friction. The infiltrative action of melting snow and ice expands and exacerbates fractures due to repeated freezing and thawing, gradually increasing crack widths and lateral displacements, thus decreasing the stability of the jointed rock mass. During the landslide, the temperature was low, and the accumulated precipitation ranged from 10 to 30 mm. The cumulative effects of these climatic factors significantly heightened the landslide risk. (5) Human activities, such as mining and road construction, may affect groundwater levels, subsequently altering geological structures and hydrological conditions, which intensifies the risk of geological hazards. It is concluded that landslide occurrence is a complex systemic process involving both internal geological conditions and long-term effects from external factors. Finally, suggestions and insights are provided from eight aspects, including organizational leadership, disaster research, and technological applications.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: Landslides are the most widespread and damaging type of geological hazard in Hunan Province, posing significant threats to public safety and the sustainable development of the economy. Previous research on their characteristics and influencing factors has not adequately filtered disaster data, resulting in less precise analysis outcomes. To further investigate the developmental characteristics of landslides in Hunan Province, clarify their formation mechanisms, and enhance geological disaster prevention and control capabilities, this study utilizes data from detailed geological hazard surveys at a scale of 1:50,000 in Hunan Province. By employing big data analysis methods such as data collection, preprocessing, mining, and presentation, a comprehensive analysis of the developmental characteristics and influencing factors of landslide hazards in Hunan Province is conducted, focusing on landslide type characteristics, morphological features, hazard characteristics, and influencing factors. The research findings indicate the following: ① Hunan Province is primarily characterized by small, semi-circular, traction-type soil landslides with prominent surface features; ② The sliding zone (surface) is mainly located at the interface between bedrock and overburden, with a predominantly arcuate shape; the sliding zone material mainly consists of soft-plastic to plastic silty clay, clay, and gravelly clay; ③ The landslides exhibit pronounced characteristics of occurrence in clusters, delay, phase, and reactivation, with severe rainfall-induced erosion and compression as the primary modes of hazard; ④ The severity level of landslide disasters and hazards is predominantly small in scale; ⑤ Various intrinsic and extrinsic influencing factors work together to trigger landslides; among these, topography and landforms, groundwater, rock and soil type, geological structure, and slope gradient are the main intrinsic factors controlling landslide development, while human engineering activities and rainfall represent the primary extrinsic triggering conditions for landslide occurrences. The research results contribute to a thorough understanding of the occurrence patterns of landslides in Hunan Province, providing a theoretical foundation for effectively preventing landslide geological disasters and offering crucial references for disaster risk management and early warning systems in the region.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: To reveal the temporal and spatial distribution patterns of landslide disasters in Sichuan Province since 2000 and to explore the factors influencing the spatial distribution of these landslides, historical landslide disaster data from 2000 to 2019 in Sichuan Province was analyzed using various spatial analysis methods, the M-K test method, and random forest analysis. The results indicated that landslide disasters in Sichuan Province exhibited a trend of first stabilizing (2000-2003), followed by rapid growth (2004-2011), and then gradually increasing (2012-2019). Seasonally, landslide occurrences were particularly pronounced from June to August each year. The distribution direction of landslide disasters shifted towards the northeast-southwest over the period from 2000 to 2011, transitioning from a concentrated to a dispersed distribution. The average centroid of landslides shifted southward and southeastward over the months. Elevation factors and human activities had the highest influence on landslide disasters, followed by maximum 3-day rainfall and annual average rainfall, while slope aspect factors had the lowest influence. These findings can provide scientific support for landslide disaster prevention and risk management.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: Landslide disasters pose a significant threat to ecosystem health, and ecological risk assessment of landslide hazards is crucial for ecosystem management and risk prevention. Based on the "probabilistic loss" theory, a comprehensive ecological risk assessment framework for landslide disasters, integrating landslide hazard, ecological vulnerability, and potential loss, has been proposed. An empirical study was conducted in the upper reaches of the Yangtze River. Landslide hazard assessment was performed using a random forest model, ecological vulnerability analysis was conducted using landscape indices, and potential loss was characterized using ecosystem service and population spatial data. The results indicate: (1) The hazard levels of landslides in the study area are predominantly very low and low, accounting for 44% and 14% of the total area, respectively; moderate hazard areas constitute 17% of the total area, primarily located in the northern part of Guizhou Province; high and very high hazard areas account for 17% and 8% of the total area, mainly distributed in Ya'an City and Bazhong City to Dazhou City in Sichuan Province, as well as in the Three Gorges Reservoir area. (2) High and very high levels of ecological vulnerability cover 40% of the total area, primarily concentrated in the Sichuan Basin, Yunnan-Guizhou Plateau, Three Gorges Reservoir area, and the plains and hilly regions of the Loess Plateau, with potential losses mainly concentrated in the Three Gorges Reservoir area and the Hengduan Mountains, which have strong ecosystem service functions but entail higher potential losses when landslide disasters occur. (3) Areas with higher ecological risk show localized clustering, covering 3.21% of the total area, mostly concentrated in the Min River, Dadu River, and Yangtze River basin regions; the causes and mechanisms of high ecological risk in the upper reaches of the Yangtze River exhibit significant spatial heterogeneity, with human activities exerting a considerable impact on ecological risk.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text:
1) Based on a detailed geohazard survey at a 1:10,000 scale in rural areas, the evaluation scope for shallow and thick accumulation layer landslides was meticulously delineated. For these two types of landslide disasters, a differentiated susceptibility evaluation index system was proposed, tailored to meet the precision requirements of rural-scale assessments. This study conducted susceptibility evaluations for various landslide types, providing a reference for detailed investigation and evaluation of landslides at the rural scale.

2) The susceptibility evaluation model was determined considering the differences in the completeness of landslide disaster samples in the study area. Due to a lack of samples for shallow accumulation layer landslides, the Analytic Hierarchy Process was employed for susceptibility evaluation. For thick accumulation layer landslides, given the limited number of samples, identified slopes exhibiting some deformation but not yet having undergone total displacement—categorized as "intermediate state" disaster samples, with state variables between 1 and 0—were included as an important supplement to the statistical model. A multi-class database for thick accumulation layer landslide samples was established, and a multi-class logistic regression model, distinct from traditional binary logistic regression, was utilized to evaluate susceptibility for thick accumulation layer landslide disasters.

3) Based on differentiated evaluation indices and models, susceptibility evaluations for both types of landslide disasters were completed. Results indicated that extremely high and high susceptibility areas for shallow accumulation layer landslides are primarily located in the northern region of the study area along the slope and in concentrated human activity zones in the central and southern areas, with 70.59% of deformation points in high and extremely high susceptibility zones. In the evaluation results for thick accumulation layer landslides, the frequency ratios for extremely high and high susceptibility zones were 2.30 and 1.24, respectively, significantly surpassing those of low susceptibility zones. The overall ROC curve's AUC value reached 0.823, demonstrating the strong rationality and effectiveness of the evaluation results obtained from the multi-class logistic regression model.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text:
Conducting a landslide susceptibility assessment is a fundamental scientific prerequisite for disaster prevention and reduction. Hanjin District in Ankang City, Shaanxi Province, is a region frequently affected by landslide disasters, providing an excellent window for landslide research. This paper takes Yinghu Town in Hanjin District as a case study, comprehensively selecting ten influencing factors: altitude, terrain undulation, slope structure, aspect, coefficient of variation of elevation, thickness of the cover layer, gradient, normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI), distance to roads, and distance to rivers. The assessment is based on slope units, and frequency ratio (FR) analysis is employed to examine the distribution characteristics of landslides. Further, based on a logistic regression-frequency ratio combination (LR-FR), a susceptibility assessment of landslides in the study area is conducted, followed by validation and analysis of results. The findings indicate that the coefficient of variation of elevation, terrain undulation, aspect, and distance to roads are the primary controlling factors influencing landslide development in the study area; the success rate and prediction rate of LR-FR are 0.804 and 0.843, respectively. Extremely high and high susceptibility zones are mainly distributed along both banks of the Yinghu Reservoir and near roads and rivers, occupying 33.66% of the total slope area, with a landslide development area of 1.08 km², which accounts for 84.36% of the total landslide area, demonstrating a good consistency with the actual distribution of landslides, indicating the reliability of the susceptibility assessment results. The research findings provide a new foundational reference for geological hazard risk control and landslide susceptibility evaluation in similar regions around Yinghu Town, Hanjin District. 

(1) The analysis results of the influencing factors and spatial distribution relationship of landslides in Yinghu Town indicate that the eastern aspect is a susceptible slope direction, the elevation range of 285-510m is prone to landslides, the parallel slope is a susceptible slope structure type, and the NDVI range of 0.201-0.222 is prone to landslide occurrence; landslide susceptibility increases with steeper slopes, and the risk of landslides also increases the closer one is to roads and rivers. 

(2) The extremely high and high susceptibility zones in Yinghu Town are located along both banks of Yinghu Reservoir and near roads and rivers, covering an area of approximately 58.23 km², which represents 33.66% of the total slope area. 

(3) Validation results indicate that the success rate and prediction rate of the model are 0.804 and 0.843, respectively, with the FR gradually decreasing from extremely high to extremely low susceptibility zones, where the actual area of developed landslides in the smaller extremely high and high susceptibility zones is 1.08 km², accounting for a significant 84.36% of the total landslide area, corroborating the effectiveness of the susceptibility assessment results. 

(4) The coefficient of variation of elevation, terrain undulation, aspect, and distance to roads are the main controlling factors for landslide development in Yinghu Town, while the slope structure and cover layer thickness factors have a relatively minor influence on landslide development in the area.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text:
**Objective:** To analyze the formation mechanisms, disintegration characteristics, and destruction mechanisms of landslide hazards in the drawdown zone of the Three Gorges Reservoir area, as well as the current research status regarding soil pollution issues, highlighting existing research deficiencies and proposing forward-looking recommendations.  
**Method:** Review relevant literature to analyze current research progress and trends.  
**Results:** The bank slopes of the Three Gorges Reservoir are characterized by a stratified fractured rock structure formed through variations in water levels, rainfall, or prolonged weathering. The water level in the drawdown zone repeatedly rises and falls, placing the mudstone in a cyclic "wet-dry" alternating environment. Soluble materials within the mudstone dissolve, reducing cementation, and leading to the gradual expansion of fractures, which results in disintegration. During changes in water levels, the landslide body involves a process of saturation-unsaturation transformation, affecting the strength of the landslide rock-soil body and local pore water pressure, ultimately triggering landslide disasters. The landslide hazards in the drawdown zone have resulted in a spatial pattern where the concentration of heavy metals in the slope soil is generally higher in the upstream and downstream of the reservoir and lower in the midstream, placing the soil at a lightly polluted level, thereby promoting potential ecological risks of organic soil pollution to the downstream ecological environment of the reservoir area. The diversity of plant species in the drawdown zone significantly decreases due to fluctuations in water levels, with herbaceous plants being predominant.  
**Conclusion:** Research on landslide hazards in the drawdown zone of the Three Gorges Reservoir is not systematic enough, requiring further investigation into the mechanisms of landslide formation, disintegration characteristics, and destruction mechanisms. The correlation between soil erosion, changes in physicochemical properties in the drawdown zone, and geological hazards remains insufficiently studied. Most studies regarding soil pollution caused by landslides in the drawdown zone have focused on current status surveys, and early research has not established authoritative reliable baseline values, leading to inadequate bases for soil pollution assessment. Research on the migration and transformation patterns of soil pollutants in the region is not yet comprehensive; attention to non-conventional pollutants, apart from routine pollutants, is inadequate. Most studies on the changes in plant communities in the region have merely described change characteristics without深入探讨 on the underlying mechanisms.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: Intense earthquakes can trigger a significant number of co-seismic landslides in mountainous areas and have a substantial impact on the development levels of geological hazards in post-earthquake disaster zones. Therefore, studying the evolution characteristics of earthquake-induced landslides plays a crucial role in the prevention and mitigation of geological hazards in regions affected by strong seismic events. This study focuses on the area of Yingxiu, which was strongly disturbed by the 2008 MS 8.0 Wenchuan earthquake. High-resolution images of the region (approximately 66 km²) were analyzed for landslide interpretation and cataloging during one pre-earthquake phase (April 2005) and five post-earthquake phases (June 2008, April 2011, April 2013, May 2015, and May 2017). Utilizing a GIS platform, data on seven primary factors—including elevation, slope gradient, slope aspect, curvature, stratigraphic lithology, distance to the nearest water system, and distance to the seismic fault—were obtained to study the long-term evolution characteristics of earthquake landslides. Additionally, a comparative analysis of landslide activity intensity across different periods was conducted to investigate the evolution patterns of landslide disasters induced by the Wenchuan earthquake in this area. Results indicate that from 2008 to 2017, the total area of landslides in the study area dramatically decreased, with co-seismic landslide area reductions from 21.41 km² to 1.33 km², suggesting that the activity level of post-earthquake landslides has recovered or approached pre-earthquake levels. Overall, the scale of landslides in the study area continued to diminish, with landslide activity decreasing over time, and the numbers of reactivated and new landslides correspondingly reduced. Regions where landslide disaster areas decreased were primarily situated at elevations of 1,000 to 2,100 m, with slope gradients of 30° to 55°, slope aspects of 40° to 180°, and curvatures ranging from -2 to 2. Furthermore, the Pengguan complex environment in the Yingxiu study area is more conducive to the occurrence of landslides, while the sedimentary clastic rock lithology favors the recovery of landslide activity. When the distance to the nearest water system exceeds 1,600 m, the effect of the water system on landslides gradually diminishes, and the study area exhibits a hanging wall effect in seismic landslides, characterized by a significantly higher number of landslides in the northwestern region of the fault compared to the southeastern side.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text:
The topography, geotechnical properties and structures, precipitation, and snowmelt create highly favorable conditions for landslide occurrence. Through field investigations and literature review, the development characteristics and disaster-forming mechanisms of the Tekesi Daban landslide were analyzed, its stability was calculated, and its mechanical formation mechanism was inferred to be of the translational type. The landslide triggering mechanisms of the primary types of geotechnical structures such as loess + mudstone, loess + bedrock (limestone), and loess + gravel + soft rock (mudstone) were revealed. For the unstable slopes in the study area, measures including internal and external wire mesh, transverse and longitudinal concrete beams, concrete covers, protective columns (with external reinforced bar spikes), and grass planting in loess for stabilization were applied. 

(1) The Tekesi Daban landslide is classified as a translational landslide. 
(2) The topography, geotechnical properties and strength, precipitation, and snowmelt provide very favorable conditions for the occurrence of landslides, which must be taken into account in disaster prevention and early warning. 
(3) The Swedish circular arc sliding method and FLAC software were used to model and analyze the stability of the eastern slope of Tekesi Daban under three conditions: dead weight, dead weight + heavy rain, and dead weight + heavy rain + earthquake. The eastern slope of Tekesi Daban is generally stable under natural conditions, unstable under heavy rain or continuous rainfall, and also unstable under the dual effects of heavy rain and earthquake. Overgrazing and human engineering activities decrease the slope stability factor, with tensile failure at the rear edge of the slope and an increasing area of shear failure at the toe of the slope. 
(4) Appropriate anti-slide piles and sheet pile walls are recommended for the landslide on the eastern side of Tekesi Daban. For the steep clastic slopes mainly composed of layered mudstone, reinforcement measures employing "internal and external wire mesh + transverse and longitudinal concrete beams + concrete covers and protective columns (with external reinforced bar spikes) + grass planting in loess" have shown favorable results.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: (1) This paper employs fractal theory and the box dimension method to investigate the distribution characteristics of landslide disaster points in the Dacha District under differing conditions of geomorphology, rainfall, and vegetation cover. By categorizing single-factor hazard levels based on the magnitude of fractal dimensions, a judgment matrix is constructed using the analytic hierarchy process to determine the weights of single-factor indicators. A multi-factor weighted superimposition model is operated in ArcGIS, and the potential hazard zones are delineated according to the natural breaks classification method. Finally, the partition results are validated using the landslide density method, with landslide density arranged in descending order according to the levels of potential hazard zones. Consequently, it can be observed that fractal theory effectively analyzes the distribution characteristics of disaster points and the natural conditions influencing their distribution, allowing for the delineation of potential hazard risk areas, thereby providing visual data support for the effective management of geological hazards. (2) In the potential hazard zones classified by representative influencing factors such as geomorphological types, annual average rainfall, and vegetation cover: (1) The high-risk zones mainly consist of low mountain and deep hill areas, with annual rainfall ranging from 1050 to 1250 mm and vegetation cover <30%; the geological structure is located at the axis and both wings of the medium mountain anticline; the strata exhibit a gently sloping west wing and a steeper east wing, with dip angles of 10°-25° and 20°-47°, respectively, where jointed and weathered fractures are well developed. This region's rock mass is highly fragmented, combined with low vegetation cover and high rainfall, making it prone to landslides. (2) The medium-risk zones have the smallest distribution area, higher rainfall, and primarily expose strata from the Upper Jurassic Suining Formation and Middle Jurassic Shaxi Temple Formation mudstones, siltstone-mudstone interbedded with sandstone, and siltstone; the strata dip gently at the axis and are steeper on the wings, with dip angles of 10°-35°. Geological conditions in this area are somewhat better, resulting in a lower likelihood of landslide disasters. (3) The low-risk zones feature the highest vegetation cover, lower rainfall, minimal topographic relief, and gently sloping strata, yielding the lowest probability of landslide disasters.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text:
In order to clarify the impact factors and susceptibility of regional landslide disasters, and to provide references for the prevention and control of landslide geological hazards, this study focuses on Huaining County, Yunnan Province. Using GIS spatial analysis technology, we analyzed the spatial distribution and triggering factors of landslides in Huaining County. Based on detailed regional geological hazard investigation data, we established a GIS disaster database and selected seven triggering factors: elevation, slope, aspect, distance to water systems, roads, fault distance, and lithology. We conducted a weighted analysis of the proportion of landslides within each category of these factors using statistical indices, ultimately determining landslide susceptibility zones and elucidating their spatial distribution characteristics. 

Results indicate: (1) The distribution characteristics of landslide disasters exhibit spatial concentration, with the highest density of disaster points located in the central region. The areas most affected, in terms of population, are situated at lower elevations such as Ningzhou Street and Tonghongdian Township. (2) In terms of triggering factors, landslide disasters predominantly occur at elevations of 1,600 to 2,300 m (76.06%), on slopes of 10° to 30° (71.83%), and are most likely to occur in easterly, northeasterly, northwesterly, and northern aspects (71.82%). The closer the landslides are to rivers, faults, and roads, the higher the likelihood of occurrence. (3) Regarding landslide susceptibility, high-susceptibility areas are mainly distributed in Ningzhou Street, Qinglong Town, and Huaxi Town; moderate-susceptibility areas are primarily in the northeast of Qinglong Town, southwest of Ningzhou Street, central Tonghongdian Yi and Miao Autonomous Township, and eastern Panxi; low-susceptibility areas are predominantly in Tonghongdian Yi and Miao Autonomous Township and Panxi Town. Landslide disasters in Huaining County show a characteristic of fewer occurrences in the east and more in the west, as well as fewer in the south and more in the north. In the future, Huaining County should focus on landslide disaster prevention in the central and western regions.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: Landslides are common geological hazards in mountainous regions, posing significant threats to socioeconomic conditions and human safety, as well as causing damage to ecosystems and thereby affecting human well-being. During the ecological risk assessment of landslides, ecosystem services represent potential losses to ecosystems, providing a more systematic and comprehensive reference for disaster prevention and reduction. The five southwestern provinces (Sichuan, Yunnan, Guizhou, Guangxi, and Chongqing) feature diverse landform types, complex lithology, and active tectonic movements, making this one of the areas in China most severely impacted by landslide disasters. This study constructs a framework, model, and indicators for assessing ecological risks associated with landslide disasters from three dimensions: hazard potential, ecosystem vulnerability, and potential ecosystem loss. The hazard potential is determined through a comprehensive analysis of factors such as geology, topography, landforms, and precipitation, and their interrelations. Vulnerability is characterized using landscape pattern indices, and potential loss is measured by ecosystem services. Consequently, the ecological risk of landslide disasters in the five southwestern provinces is evaluated. The results indicate that areas with high potential ecosystem loss are primarily distributed south of the Ailao Mountains in Yunnan, in the Qionglai Mountains, within the Hengduan Mountains, in the Dadu River basin, northern Guangxi, and east of the Dayao Mountains. High ecological risk zones for landslides in the study area are mainly located in the Min Mountains, Qionglai Mountains, Wuliang Mountains, Ailao Mountains, Miao Mountains, Leigong Mountains, and the Dadu River basin, as well as in the region of the three parallel rivers. In terms of elevation distribution, the altitudinal range of 500 to 1500 m is where the main concentration of high-risk areas exists, accounting for 37.9% of the high-risk area. Regarding ecosystem types, high-risk areas are predominantly forest ecosystems, constituting 66.4% of the total area of high-risk zones. There should be strengthened monitoring and early warning systems for landslides in high ecological risk areas, with an emphasis on enhancing ecosystem protection in these regions to improve the stability and resilience of ecosystems.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: This article focuses on the assessment of landslide susceptibility in a specific region. By summarizing previous research experiences and findings, the study targets the Longju Dam area within the Modiaoxi watershed in the upper reaches of the Yangtze River and encompasses a thorough analysis, leading to the following main conclusions: (1) The application of the ArcGIS system is fundamental to data management and final mapping in regional landslide susceptibility assessments. The ArcGIS system requires data collection, high-precision remote sensing interpretation, and field investigations, alongside essential steps such as establishing a basic database, processing spatial data, and conducting spatial data analysis. (2) The spatial distribution patterns of landslides have been summarized. The development of landslides in the study area is primarily controlled by three factors: ① The engineering geological conditions of exposed strata, with the Triassic Badong Formation, Jurassic Lower Shaximiao Formation, and Jurassic Upper Shaximiao Formation being the most prone to landslides. ② Tectonic locations, where numerous landslides are found within the core of the Longju Dam anticline and the transition zone between the Ganchang syncline and Longju Dam anticline. ③ Micro-topographic features are also crucial influences on the spatial distribution of landslides, with landslides occurring more frequently on convex slopes, ascending slopes, and step-like slopes. (3) An evaluation of landslide susceptibility in the study area was conducted. Utilizing the BP neural network model for assessment, the landslide susceptibility in the study area is categorized into four levels: low susceptibility area (0~0.3), comprising 66.77% of slope units; medium susceptibility area (0.3~0.5), making up 8.32% of slope units; high susceptibility area (0.5~0.7), representing 19.84% of slope units; and very high susceptibility area (>0.7), accounting for 5.07% of slope units.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: (1) Slope, hydrological networks, stratigraphic lithology, road proximity, and elevation are the primary factors influencing landslide and collapse disasters, with a cumulative contribution rate exceeding 80%. (2) The high-sensitivity ranges for the five main factors are found in areas with a slope of ≥30°, a distance of 200 to 800 m from hydrological networks, Quaternary, Jurassic, and Late Precambrian strata, a road distance of ≤2,000 m, and an elevation range of 300 to 700 m. (3) High susceptibility areas are mainly located in Maguizhen, Shenshui Village, Sanjia Village, and Liutang Village, with widespread settlement distribution and severe disasters. It is recommended to strengthen community monitoring and prevention efforts, as well as professional monitoring, to effectively carry out disaster prevention and reduction work. Identifying the primary controlling factors, high-sensitivity ranges, and susceptibility zoning for landslide disasters in the study area can guide local residents in their production activities, economic development planning, and disaster risk management, holding significant engineering practical implications. (1) Based on remote sensing interpretation of past landslides and field investigations, GIS and the deterministic coefficient method were used to calculate the CF values of eight influencing factors affecting landslide disasters in the study area, leading to the identification of high-sensitivity ranges for each factor related to landslide occurrence. (2) A principal component analysis was conducted on various indicators, revealing the main factors affecting landslide disasters in the region (with a cumulative contribution rate greater than 80%) and their weights; building on this, overlay analysis yielded the susceptibility zoning for disasters in the study area; the high-susceptibility zones correspond well with locations of previous landslides, demonstrating reasonable and effective susceptibility zoning. (3) Areas most prone to landslides are primarily found in Maguizhen, Shenshui Village, Sanjia Village, and Liutang Village, characterized by a high density of settlements and severe hazards. It is suggested to enhance community monitoring and prevention as well as specialized monitoring to minimize casualty and economic losses brought about by disasters.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: In summary, a comparison of the seismic geological hazards in the Ludian and Jinggu seismic zones reveals that the Ludian Ms6.5 earthquake triggered a large-scale seismic geological disaster, resulting in significant casualties, whereas the Jinggu Ms6.6 earthquake, which also occurred in mountainous terrain, induced relatively lighter seismic geological hazards. The analysis indicates that the substantial differences can be attributed to the following factors: (1) Differences in the human environment. The Ludian seismic zone has a high population density and frequent human activities, including extensive engineering projects such as railway and road construction, hillside housing, and factory development, which have disrupted natural slope stability, causing high embankments. Deforestation has further degraded slope protection, facilitating rainwater infiltration and triggering landslides. The interaction of anthropogenic activities with natural disasters such as earthquakes enhances the likelihood of earthquake-induced landslides. (2) Differences in natural geographical conditions. The Ludian seismic zone is located in the northeastern Yunnan Wumeng mountainous region, characterized by towering mountains and significant elevation changes. The interannual temperature variation is substantial, with summer temperatures exceeding 35°C and winter temperatures around -15°C. Exogenic processes include not only erosion and weathering by water but also glacial erosion. In addition to chemical actions, physical weathering processes such as thermal weathering and freeze-thaw action are prevalent. Geologically, the area is dominated by easily weathered Permian Emei Group basalt and prominently exposed carbonate rocks, with strong karst processes. Deep-cut cliffs and nearly vertical riverbank landscapes characterize the valley, resulting in high terrain contrast, steep slopes, rapid river flows, sparse vegetation, and exposed bedrock. In contrast, the Jinggu region experiences a subtropical mountainous monsoon climate, primarily influenced by chemical weathering, with weaker physical weathering processes. The lithology mainly consists of Mesozoic sandstones and mudstones, exhibiting strong weathering resistance and negligible solubility. Lush vegetation with approximately 75% forest coverage dominates the area, with the main exogenic processes being fluvial action and chemical weathering, while the overall weathering intensity is relatively low. (3) Differences in geological structural background. Both Ludian and Jinggu seismic zones are located in seismically active areas with intense tectonic activity. The former is situated within the Zhaotong to Ludian active fault zone and closer to the leading edge of the secondary active block of the Liangshan region, where tectonic stress is relatively high. The latter lies within the Wuliangshan active fault zone, farther from the Tibetan Plateau. However, this does not inherently explain the differences in the geological disasters triggered by the two earthquakes. In conclusion, the significant disparities in landslide occurrences from the Ludian Ms6.5 and Jinggu Ms6.6 earthquakes stem from variations in human environment, lithology, topography, and vegetation. For the Ludian seismic zone, the combination of substantial elevation differences, widespread basalt and carbonate rock distribution, along with strong fluvial erosion, freeze-thaw processes, and chemical weathering, are the primary factors contributing to the frequent occurrence of earthquake-induced landslides.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: This study, based on interpreted remote sensing imagery, utilizes MAPGIS spatial analysis methods to investigate the spatial development patterns of landslide hazards triggered by the Wenchuan earthquake within An County. The main conclusions are as follows: 1) Due to the peak ground acceleration being higher on the hanging wall than on the footwall during the reverse faulting event, the density of landslide hazard points in the hanging wall areas of the Beichuan-Yingxiu Fault and the Pengxian-Guangyuan Fault is greater than that in the footwall. Additionally, as the primary fault of the Wenchuan earthquake, the Beichuan-Yingxiu Fault exhibited significantly more activity during this earthquake than the Pengxian-Guangyuan Fault, with landslide point density values on both its hanging and footwalls far exceeding those of the Pengxian-Guangyuan Fault. 2) Influenced by both the Beichuan-Yingxiu Fault and the Pengxian-Guangyuan Fault and controlled by the locking effect, the relationship between landslide point density and distance from the seismic fault in the study area exhibits unique characteristics, as landslide point density does not significantly decrease with increasing distance from the seismic fault, with a maximum density occurring at distances of 2 to 6 km from the fault. 3) The processes of downcutting and lateral erosion by rivers create effective free faces for landslide formation, which further fragment the riverbank strata, enhancing the rock unloading capacity, leading to the characteristic linear distribution of landslide hazards along water systems. 4) As the earthquake intensity increases and the actual ground damage intensifies, the density of landslide hazard points increases with the intensity level. 5) Rock masses that are easily fragmented into debris are more conducive to landslide hazard formation; thus, as rock hardness decreases, the degree of landslide development shows an increasing trend.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text: The Jinsha River is the upstream section of the Yangtze River, located at the transitional zone between the southeastern edge of the Tibetan Plateau and the Yungui Plateau and Sichuan Basin. The geological environment in this area exhibits significant variability, with strong neotectonic movements and a fragile ecological environment, resulting in frequent landslide disasters. To study the development, distribution characteristics, and influencing factors of landslide disasters in the upper reaches of the Jinsha River, the Suwalong-Benzi River section was selected as a case study. Through field surveys and the collection of existing data, a statistical analysis was conducted on 32 landslide disasters that developed in this river section. The analysis results indicate that: 1. The spatial distribution of landslide disasters is uneven, mainly concentrated in four areas along the Suwalong-Benzi River section: Wangdalong, Maoding, Simuda on the mainstream Jinsha River, and the ancient academic town of Dingquhe on its tributary; 2. The predominant type of landslide disaster consists of giant, large-scale deep-seated rockslides, with fewer occurrences of landslides in Quaternary deposits; 3. The landslides in the Suwalong-Benzi River section originated relatively early, and coupled with strong neotectonic movements and rapid river incision, they exhibit a characteristic where the elevation of the landslide scarp is significantly higher than the river water level; 4. Landslide disasters are closely related to geological environmental conditions such as topography, lithology, geological structure, and bank slope structure in this river section, with the greatest susceptibility to landslides occurring in mountainous areas with a slope range of 30°-35°, in green schist and ophiolite formations, within a 1 km range of fault zones, and under conditions of steep bank slopes.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text:
1) The slope in the Longjiapo landslide area is an inclined slope, with the rock layers dipping towards the downstream side and slightly into the slope. The landslide area is primarily characterized by two sets of joints: 305°∠70° and 31°∠75°. These two sets of nearly vertical joint planes cut through the bedrock of the landslide area, resulting in a wide and deep extent of highly weathered rock masses. In steeper areas, local collapses are easily triggered, and the debris from these collapses is trapped by the rock mass on the gently sloping side, gradually forming a certain thickness of colluvial deposits on the slope, providing conditions for landslide material sources. a. The fill on the left side of the landslide first becomes unstable; b. The main sliding mass becomes unstable due to lateral shear disturbances. 

2) The Longjiapo landslide occurs on an inclined slope within the gently dipping rock structure, with a ridge extending on either side of the main sliding zone (Ridge 1 and Ridge 2). This slope structure should generally be relatively stable. However, due to the dip of the rock layers towards the downstream side, the accumulations on the slope exhibit a tendency to push towards the downstream side. 

3) The river on the SW side of the main sliding zone has a strong erosive effect on the foot of the slope, causing localized depressions that form negative topography, thereby providing relatively favorable conditions for the landslide to propagate towards the SW side. 

4) After a large volume of excavated soil was deposited in the initiation zone, the groundwater runoff pathways that could drain through this negative topography were blocked, leading to groundwater accumulation in the main sliding zone after rainfall. The lubricating and softening effects of the water reduce the shear strength of the potential slip surface, decreasing the sliding resistance of the sliding mass. Under the loading effect of the upper fill, sliding initially occurs in the initiation zone. 

5) The sliding damage in the initiation zone exerts strong lateral shear disturbances on the main sliding zone, altering the conditions at the front edge of the main sliding zone, ultimately resulting in the overall instability of the main sliding zone. 

6) Landslides induced by lateral shear disturbances are a particularly subtle type of landslide. In conducting slope hazard assessments and early warning work, it is essential to thoroughly investigate the geological environment surrounding the potential hazard body and identify disturbance factors at the lateral boundaries, rather than solely focusing on the geotechnical characteristics of the internal soil and rock mass.

major categories: Slope rock and soil mass movement hazards
text:
On January 11, 2013, a landslide occurred in Zhenxiong, Yunnan, with a sliding distance of nearly 800 meters and a deposit volume of approximately 40×10^4 m³. Over 60 houses in the Zhaojiagou village group were destroyed, resulting in 46 fatalities. This paper investigates the causes of the significant Zhaojiagou landslide disaster, which is rare during the dry season, through on-site surveys and employs the finite element method to analyze the instability mechanism of the landslide under prolonged rainfall and mining conditions. Geologically, the landslide is situated in the coal-bearing strata of the Wumeng mountainous area, with steep Triassic Middle Member limestone and dolomite at the top, relatively steep Triassic Lower Member sandstone and shale in the middle, and gently inclined Permian Upper Member shale and mudstone at the base, which locally contains coal. This setting creates an engineering geological structure characterized by hard strata above and soft strata below, along with a hydrogeological structure that is rich in water above and impermeable below, making it prone to landslide geological hazards.

Topographically, the area resembles a boot-shaped landform, where the steep upper terrain predisposes the slope to instability, while the open and expansive mid-lower valley provides conditions for long-distance movement, facilitating the transformation of potential energy into kinetic energy, and thus favoring rapid long-distance sliding, leading to severe losses. The landslide can be categorized into three parts: the source area, the scarp and accumulation area, and the disaster-affected area. The rapidly moving landslide mass directly covered several houses in the Zhaojiagou village group, while the dispersed landslide materials flowed and buried multiple dwellings along the gently sloping valley, becoming a significant cause of the major disaster.

Finite element simulation results indicate that a rise in the groundwater level within the accumulation layer can reduce the stability coefficient of the Zhaojiagou landslide by more than 10%, suggesting that persistent light rainfall can trigger instability in residual slope deposits located in steep valley settings. Due to the relatively thin coal seams below the landslide, with an intact overburden and a thickness exceeding 200 meters, the small coal mine excavations during the 1960s and 1970s had no significant impact on landslide deformation and instability. Addressing the social issues arising from this catastrophic landslide, the author recommends enhancing public crisis management for significant geological disasters, improving early identification and risk management of high-velocity long-distance landslides in coal-bearing strata areas, and strengthening professional training in the prevention of complex geological hazards.
